items.question: Which Gradle file in an Android Studio project is used to specify app-specific dependencies, SDK versions (compileSdk, minSdk, targetSdk), and build types?
items.explanation: The module-level build.gradle file (often labeled 'Module: app') is where dependencies and specific Android configuration settings are defined.

items.question: What is the primary conceptual difference between an Explicit Intent and an Implicit Intent?
items.answer: Explicit Intents name a specific target component, while Implicit Intents specify a general action to be performed.
items.explanation: Explicit intents designate the target component by its name, whereas implicit intents specify an action that allows the system to find a suitable app.
choices: Explicit Intents are only for system apps, while Implicit are for third-party apps.
Explicit Intents name a specific target component, while Implicit Intents specify a general action to be performed.
Implicit Intents are faster to execute than Explicit Intents.
Explicit Intents do not require any declarations in the AndroidManifest.xml.

items.question: Which higher-level feature of Gradle allows it to automatically fetch and integrate external libraries like 'commons-text' into your project?
items.explanation: Gradle's dependency management system integrates external libraries by automatically fetching them from repositories like Maven Central.

items.question: Write the Kotlin code to launch an activity named 'SecondActivity' using an explicit intent.
items.explanation: To start a specific internal activity, you create an Intent object with the context and the target class, then call startActivity.

items.question: Why is it considered a best practice to call `resolveActivity()` before calling `startActivity()` with an implicit intent?
items.answer: To prevent the app from crashing if no activity on the device can handle the intent.
items.explanation: If `startActivity()` is called for an implicit intent that cannot be resolved by any app on the device, the application will crash.
choices: To speed up the performance of the intent launch.
To ensure the intent has the correct permissions to run.
To prevent the app from crashing if no activity on the device can handle the intent.
To allow the user to select their favorite app automatically.

items.question: Identify the error in this `AndroidManifest.xml` snippet that prevents 'ShareActivity' from being launched by other apps via an implicit intent.
items.explanation: The `android:exported` attribute must be set to 'true' for a component to be launchable by components from outside your app.

items.question: Write the Kotlin code to open a website (e.g., 'https://www.google.com') using an implicit intent.
items.answer:
val intent = Intent(Intent.ACTION_VIEW)
intent.data = Uri.parse("https://www.google.com")
startActivity(intent)
items.explanation: Using ACTION_VIEW with a URL data URI allows the Android system to find and open a web browser app.

items.question: In the target activity, which method is used to retrieve a String value passed via `putExtra("key", "value")`?
items.explanation: The `getStringExtra` method is used on the intent object in the target activity to retrieve string data.

items.question: Spot the syntax error in this `build.gradle` dependency declaration.
items.answer: Missing the version number for the dependency.
items.explanation: Gradle dependencies require a group, name, and a specific version (e.g., '1.13.0') to be successfully integrated.

items.question: Which `<intent-filter>` component describes the type of data the activity is capable of handling, such as a specific URL scheme or a MIME type?
items.explanation: The `<data>` tag is used within an intent filter to specify data details like schemes, hosts, and MIME types.

items.question: Implement a code snippet for 'Activity A' to start 'Activity B' while passing a 'user_id' (Integer) and 'session_token' (String).
items.answer:
val intent = Intent(this, ActivityB::class.java)
intent.putExtra("user_id", 123)
intent.putExtra("session_token", "abc-xyz")
startActivity(intent)
items.explanation: The `putExtra` method is overloaded to handle various types like Int and String, allowing multiple pieces of data to be passed simultaneously.

items.question: Which Intent action is specifically used to share data (like text or an image) to another app via a chooser?
items.explanation: ACTION_SEND is the standard action for sharing data with other apps on the device.

items.question: A developer wants an activity to be the main entry point of the app shown in the launcher. What is missing or incorrect in this declaration?
items.answer: The action should be 'MAIN' and the category should be 'LAUNCHER'.
items.explanation: For an activity to appear in the app launcher, it must have the 'android.intent.action.MAIN' action and the 'android.intent.category.LAUNCHER' category.

items.question: Write the code for an implicit intent that shares the text 'Check out this app!' and provides a title for the app chooser.
items.answer:
val sendIntent = Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND)
sendIntent.type = "text/plain"
sendIntent.putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TEXT, "Check out this app!")
val shareIntent = Intent.createChooser(sendIntent, "Share via")
startActivity(shareIntent)
items.explanation: Using `Intent.createChooser` ensures the user is prompted with a list of apps that can handle the SEND action, even if a default is already set.

items.question: In the context of manual compilation, what happens if the 'classpath' (-cp) is not correctly specified when running a Kotlin app that uses external JARs?
items.answer: The JVM will fail to find the external classes, resulting in errors.
items.explanation: When running manually, you must explicitly include all required external libraries in the classpath so the system can locate the code.
choices: Gradle will automatically download the missing JARs at runtime.
The JVM will fail to find the external classes, resulting in errors.
The application will run but will be significantly slower.
The compiler will ignore the external dependencies and continue.

items.question: Correct the missing parts of this Intent Filter meant to open the app when a user clicks a 'https://www.example.com' link.
items.explanation: The `<data>` tag with scheme and host is required to limit the intent filter to only handle specific URLs.

items.question: Write the Kotlin code to define a 'send' function that uses a block of 'apply' to set the type, extra email, and extra subject for an ACTION_SEND intent.
items.explanation: Scope functions like 'apply' provide a clean way to configure multiple properties and extras on an Intent object.

items.question: Which Gradle file is used to list all modules included in the project?
items.explanation: The `settings.gradle` (or `settings.gradle.kts`) file manages the overall project structure by defining which modules are included in the build.
